Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The President Trump's re-election campaign is suing CNN for libel.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: This follows similar lawsuits against the New York Times and the Washington Post.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: All three suits targeting opinion pieces.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But some legal experts say the moves are off base.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Jenna Ellis, Trump 2020 senior legal advisor, constitutional law attorney, joins us.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So Jenna, the complaint focuses on a CNN opinion story by a former federal election commission,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: General Counsel Larry Noble, he wrote a piece titled, quote, soliciting, dirt on your opponents

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: from a foreign government is a crime, malicious of charge, Trump campaign officials with it.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Why are you guys suing over this?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well we're suing CNN as well as the New York Times and Washington Post for false and

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: defamatory statements.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And CNN in particular claimed that the campaign had assessed the potential risks and benefits

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: of again soliciting Russia's help in the 2020 election and decided to leave that option

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: on the table.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: That is a false and defamatory statement that CNN knew at the time that it published the

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: statement was false.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: There was an extensive record of the campaign disavowing and disclaiming any interactions

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: of these sorts with Russia.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Did they put that in the story?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Did they put your side of the story any place?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: They didn't.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And in fact, CNN, we reached out to CNN and asked them to retract and apologize and they declined

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to and they refused.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So the campaign has been left with no option but to file a lawsuit to get the public facts

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: on the record and also to make sure that readers understand the truth.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And to seek damages that are in the millions of dollars for these types of false statements.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The Supreme Court, as you know, has said you need to show malicious intent that there were

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: false as a reckless disregard for the truth.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, it's an opinion piece.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Critics would say it's supported by the First Amendment.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Let me read you something from the Freedom Forum Institute.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: They say, quote, an opinion is not liable.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Library refers to specific facts that can be proved untrue.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: A public figure has to prove the publisher of the false statements acted with actual mouse.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It means the publisher either knew the statements or false or acted with the reckless disregard

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: for whether they were false.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's a lot harder to prove than negligence.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Do you think you got those already in the bag?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It is harder to prove, but of course, we look forward to proving that at trial.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And of course, CNN and these other publications knew that their false statements were false

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: at the time that they published them and published them with intentional disregard for the truth.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so, here, although this was published in the context of an opinion piece, the court

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: has also had a long precedent of case law that just because you couch a statement as opinion

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: doesn't mean that you can assert and make false statements a fact.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The court will look at each individual statement and determine, is this a statement of opinion

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: or is this an assertion a fact?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And here in the CNN, the New York Times and the Washington Post pieces, all of their statements

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: are provably false and their factual statements that were absolutely false and defamatory.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Now would you respond to those that think that the president is trying to and the campaign

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: is trying to muzzle media outlets, they're not like.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Not at all.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so, this is the campaign that's filing these suits, not the president.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And also, the freedom of press, we, of course, value and we have the freest, fairest press,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: but that doesn't shield the press to make false statements.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so, of course, that's why the law allows for libel suits and to hold these media outlets

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: responsible when they publish false statements because the public deserves to have the truth

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and not these false statements.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so, we have to go to court because they're unwilling to retract these statements.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You just said, you know, we had the freest, what fairest press, but you know, the president

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: calls on the enemy to the people during his rally, as he points out to all the cameras behind

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the audience and slams the media.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, the law, so the freest, fairest press in the sense that the law and the First Amendment

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: is absolutely fair to the press.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It doesn't mean that the press itself has been fair to the campaign or to the president.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Clearly, here you have three giant media outlets that have published with reckless disregard

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: for the truth.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so, they need to be held accountable as well, and their readers should not be intentionally

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: misinformed.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Do you see an end?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Is not publicly commenting on this quickly?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: What do you think is going to happen?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, we look forward to proving this at trial, and I think that we will absolutely

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: prevail.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: All right, Jenna.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: We'll see you as we go through the courts.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Thank you so much.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Thank you for covering this area.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Of course.
